3.1.1

Size and Types of Firms

Firms vary enormously in size, legal structure, and ownership — and these differences shape how a firm can raise finance, how much risk its owners bear, and how it is likely to behave.

Sole Trader
A business owned and run by one person, who keeps all profits but bears unlimited liability for the firm's debts.
Partnership
A business owned by two or more people who share profits, decision-making, and (typically) unlimited liability for the firm's debts.
Private Limited Company (Ltd)
A company whose shares are owned privately and cannot be sold on a public stock exchange; owners (shareholders) have limited liability, meaning they can only lose what they invested.
Public Limited Company (plc)
A company whose shares can be bought and sold by the public on a stock exchange, allowing it to raise large amounts of capital, but exposing it to takeover and requiring greater public disclosure.
Limited Liability
A legal protection meaning an owner's personal assets cannot be seized to pay off business debts beyond the amount they invested — a major incentive to incorporate as a limited company.
Measure of Firm SizeExplanationLimitation
Number of employeesA simple, widely available measure.Ignores capital-intensive firms that generate huge output/revenue with relatively few staff.
Market capitalisationShare price × number of shares in issue.Only applies to publicly listed companies, and fluctuates with investor sentiment rather than underlying output.
Annual turnover (revenue)Total sales revenue in a given year.A high-turnover, low-margin firm (e.g. a supermarket) can appear larger than a smaller but highly profitable firm.
Market shareThe proportion of total industry sales a firm accounts for.Depends heavily on how the relevant market is defined.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Why Small Firms Continue to Survive Alongside Giants
The overwhelming majority of UK businesses are small or medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), and many survive successfully even in industries dominated by giants. Common reasons include: acting as a specialist sub-contractor or supplier to larger firms (particularly in construction and software development); serving a small, niche market with low price elasticity of demand and high income elasticity, allowing a healthy profit margin on a small volume; deliberately avoiding the internal diseconomies of scale (Section 9) that can affect very large firms; being run as a "lifestyle business" where the owner prioritises independence and work-life balance over maximum growth; and benefiting from e-commerce platforms that have lowered the barriers to entry that once favoured only large retailers.
Why Legal Structure Matters for Business Behaviour: a sole trader bearing unlimited liability has a strong personal incentive to avoid excessive risk, while a plc's shareholders, protected by limited liability, may be willing to back riskier strategies since their maximum loss is capped at their investment — a distinction that connects directly to the principal-agent problem examined in Section 6.
3.1.2

Business Growth: Organic and External Growth

Firms can grow in two fundamentally different ways: by expanding their own existing operations, or by combining with another firm entirely.

Organic (Internal) Growth
Growth achieved by expanding a firm's own existing operations — opening new stores, increasing production capacity, or launching new products — without combining with another firm.
External Growth (Merger/Acquisition)
Growth achieved by combining with, or taking over, another firm — typically faster than organic growth, but carrying integration risk.
Type of MergerDefinitionUK ExampleMain Motive
Horizontal integrationA merger between two firms at the same stage of production, in the same industry.Two rival supermarket chains merging.Increased market share and market power; potential economies of scale.
Vertical integration (forward)A merger with a firm further along the supply chain, closer to the final consumer.A clothing manufacturer acquiring a chain of retail stores.Greater control over distribution and route to market.
Vertical integration (backward)A merger with a firm further back in the supply chain, closer to raw materials.A coffee retailer acquiring coffee bean plantations.Greater control over input supply, cost, and quality.
Conglomerate mergerA merger between firms in entirely unrelated industries.A tobacco company acquiring a food and drinks business.Diversification of risk across unrelated markets.

Advantages of Growth

  • Access to economies of scale (Section 9), lowering unit costs.
  • Increased market power, potentially allowing higher prices or better terms with suppliers.
  • Diversification (particularly via conglomerate mergers) can spread risk across different markets.
  • External growth can be significantly faster than organic growth, valuable in fast-moving markets.

Disadvantages of Growth

  • Risk of internal diseconomies of scale (Section 9) as a firm becomes harder to manage and coordinate.
  • Merger integration risk: combining different corporate cultures, IT systems, and management structures often proves more difficult than anticipated.
  • Regulatory risk: significant mergers can be blocked or restricted by competition authorities (Section 25).
  • Loss of the flexibility and close customer relationships that smaller firms often enjoy.
Why Many Mergers Fail to Deliver Expected Benefits: a substantial body of business research finds that many mergers ultimately destroy shareholder value rather than creating it, largely due to underestimated integration costs and cultural clashes between the merging organisations — a reminder that the theoretical benefits of external growth (economies of scale, market power) are not automatic, and depend heavily on how well the merger is actually executed.
3.1.2

Constraints on Business Growth

Not every firm grows without limit — a range of factors can constrain how large a firm becomes, helping to explain why small firms persist even in industries where large-scale production appears to offer clear advantages.

Constraint on GrowthExplanation
Size of the marketA firm serving a small, niche, or geographically limited market may have little scope for further growth without changing its product entirely.
Access to financeGrowth, whether organic or external, typically requires significant capital; firms unable to access affordable finance (particularly smaller firms without a track record) may be constrained regardless of their ambitions.
Owner objectivesSome business owners deliberately choose not to pursue maximum growth, prioritising independence, control, or a manageable workload instead (Section 5).
Regulatory constraintsCompetition authorities can block mergers that would excessively reduce competition (Section 25), directly limiting external growth.
Diseconomies of scaleBeyond a certain size, growth can raise average costs rather than lower them (Section 9), removing much of the economic incentive to expand further.
Exam Application: when asked why a specific industry contains a mix of very large and very small firms, always consider both sides: what specifically allows large firms to grow (economies of scale, access to finance, market power) and what specifically constrains or benefits the smaller firms that persist alongside them (niche markets, avoiding diseconomies of scale, owner objectives) — the strongest answers explain the coexistence, not just one side of it.
3.1.3

Demergers

A demerger is the reverse of the growth process examined in Sections 2–3: a firm splits into two or more separate, independent businesses.

Demerger
The separation of a single business into two or more independent companies, each typically listed and traded separately, reversing a previous merger or simply splitting off part of an existing conglomerate.
Reason for DemergerExplanation
Unlocking shareholder valueInvestors and analysts may struggle to accurately value a complex conglomerate, and a demerger can allow each resulting business to be valued more clearly and attract investors specifically interested in that sector.
Removing internal diseconomies of scaleSplitting an overly large, difficult-to-manage organisation into smaller, more focused units can improve coordination and communication (Section 9).
Sharper management focusSeparate management teams can focus entirely on their own business's strategy, rather than dividing attention across unrelated divisions.
Regulatory requirementCompetition authorities can, in some circumstances, require a firm to divest part of its business as a condition of approving a merger, or as a remedy for anti-competitive behaviour (Section 25).
★ UK EXAMPLE: GSK's Consumer Healthcare Demerger
GlaxoSmithKline (GSK) demerged its consumer healthcare division — the business behind well-known brands — into a separately listed company (Haleon) in 2022. The stated rationale was that GSK's pharmaceutical and consumer healthcare businesses had fundamentally different growth profiles, risk characteristics, and investor bases, meaning both businesses could likely be managed more effectively, and valued more clearly by the stock market, once separated from one another.
Demergers as Evidence Against Unlimited Growth Benefits: the existence of demergers is itself useful evaluative evidence when discussing the case for business growth (Section 2) — if growth and diversification always improved efficiency and value, demergers would rarely make commercial sense, so their continued occurrence supports the idea that internal diseconomies of scale (Section 9) and unfocused management are genuine practical risks of unchecked growth, not merely theoretical possibilities.
3.2.1

Profit Maximisation and Alternative Objectives

Traditional economic theory assumes firms exist to maximise profit — but real firms often pursue other objectives instead, or alongside profit, with significant implications for their pricing and output decisions.

Profit Maximisation
The traditional assumed objective of a firm: setting output at the level where marginal revenue equals marginal cost (MR=MC), maximising the gap between total revenue and total cost.
Revenue (Sales) Maximisation
Setting output at the level where marginal revenue equals zero (MR=0), maximising total revenue rather than profit — output is higher, and price lower, than under profit maximisation.
Growth Maximisation
Prioritising the long-run growth of the firm (market share, scale, sales volume) even at the expense of short-run profit, often to build scale, market power, or prestige.
Satisficing
Setting a target level of profit judged "good enough" to satisfy shareholders and stakeholders, rather than pursuing the theoretical maximum — freeing management to pursue other goals (growth, work-life balance, ethical objectives) alongside an acceptable profit.
ObjectiveOutput Level (Relative to Profit Max)Why a Firm Might Pursue It
Profit maximisationBaseline (MR=MC)Owner-managed firms with a direct personal stake in profit; shareholder pressure in a plc.
Revenue maximisationHigher output, lower price (MR=0)Managers whose pay or status is linked to sales volume or market share rather than profit directly.
Growth maximisationHigher output, potentially below-cost pricing in the short runFirms in fast-growing markets prioritising long-run market position over near-term profit (e.g. early-stage tech firms).
SatisficingBelow profit-maximising outputOwners prioritising manageable workload, ethical considerations, or stakeholder relationships alongside acceptable profit.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Growth Maximisation in Early-Stage Technology Firms
Many technology and platform businesses have deliberately prioritised rapid user growth and market share over near-term profitability in their early years, often operating at a loss for extended periods, on the strategic judgement that establishing a dominant market position first would allow far greater profit once the market matured and network effects (companion Market Structures material) made their position difficult to challenge.
Why Alternative Objectives Matter for Predicting Firm Behaviour: the objective a firm actually pursues directly changes the predictions economic models make about its price and output — a revenue-maximising firm will set a lower price and higher output than a profit-maximising firm facing identical costs and demand, which matters enormously when assessing real-world pricing behaviour against the profit-maximising benchmark used throughout Section 3.4's market structure models.
3.2.1

The Principal-Agent Problem

In a large modern firm, the people who own the business (shareholders) are typically not the same people who run it day to day (managers) — a separation that can create a conflict of interest known as the principal-agent problem.

Divorce of Ownership from Control
A situation, typical of large plcs, in which the firm's owners (shareholders) delegate day-to-day decision-making to professional managers, who may not share the owners' objectives.
Principal-Agent Problem
A conflict of interest that arises when an agent (a manager, acting on behalf of others) has different objectives from the principal (the shareholder-owners) they are meant to serve, and the principal cannot perfectly monitor or control the agent's actions.
1
Shareholders (principals) want maximum long-run profit and share value

This is their financial interest as owners of the business.

2
Managers (agents) may instead be motivated by their own salary, bonuses, job security, or status

A manager's personal incentives are not automatically identical to the shareholders' interest in maximum profit — for example, a manager may prefer to expand the firm's size (raising their own status and pay) even if this does not maximise profit (Section 5's growth maximisation).

3
Shareholders cannot perfectly monitor managers' day-to-day decisions

This information asymmetry between principal and agent allows managers some scope to pursue their own objectives rather than shareholders' objectives, at least until poor performance becomes visible.

Mechanism to Reduce the Principal-Agent ProblemExplanation
Performance-related pay and share optionsLinking managers' own pay directly to profit or share price performance aligns their personal incentive with shareholders' interest.
The threat of takeoverA poorly managed, underperforming firm becomes a cheaper target for takeover by another firm, whose new management may replace the underperforming team — a market discipline on managerial behaviour.
Independent non-executive directorsBoard members without day-to-day management responsibilities can scrutinise and challenge executive decisions on behalf of shareholders.
Reporting and disclosure requirementsRegulatory requirements for plcs to publish detailed financial accounts improve shareholders' ability to monitor management performance.
Why This Connects Directly to Section 1's Legal Structures: the principal-agent problem is far more significant in large plcs with widely dispersed shareholders (each holding too small a stake to closely monitor management individually) than in a sole trader or owner-managed private company, where the owner and the day-to-day decision-maker are frequently the same person — directly linking a firm's legal structure to the scale of this particular market imperfection.
3.3.1

Revenue

Revenue is the total income a firm receives from selling its output, and understanding its three key measures — total, average, and marginal revenue — is essential for analysing pricing and output decisions across every market structure in Section 3.4.

Total Revenue (TR)
The total income a firm receives from sales, calculated as price × quantity sold (TR = P × Q).
Average Revenue (AR)
Revenue per unit sold, calculated as TR ÷ Q — for a firm with no price discrimination, AR is identical to the price charged, and AR is therefore the same curve as the demand curve.
Marginal Revenue (MR)
The additional revenue generated by selling one more unit of output.

Worked Example — TR, AR and MR

Price (£)QuantityTR (£)AR (£)MR (£)
1011010—
921898
832486
742874
653062

Key point: because the firm must lower price to sell more (a downward-sloping demand curve), MR falls faster than AR and eventually turns negative — selling an additional unit can reduce total revenue once the price cut needed to sell it outweighs the revenue gained from the extra unit itself.

Fig 1: The relationship between AR, MR and TR. TR rises while demand is elastic (MR positive), reaches a maximum exactly where MR=0 (unit elastic point), and falls once demand becomes inelastic (MR negative) — directly linking a firm's revenue curves to the price elasticity of demand concept.
Why AR = Demand: since every point on the demand curve shows the price consumers are willing to pay for a given quantity, and AR is simply revenue per unit (which equals price, absent price discrimination), the AR curve and the demand curve are one and the same — a frequently tested link between Theme 1's demand analysis and Theme 3's revenue theory.
3.3.2

Costs

A firm's costs of production determine, alongside its revenue, whether and how much profit it makes — and behave very differently in the short run, when at least one factor of production is fixed, versus the long run, when all factors can vary.

Fixed Costs (FC)
Costs that do not vary with the level of output in the short run (e.g. rent, insurance) — a firm incurs these even if it produces nothing.
Variable Costs (VC)
Costs that vary directly with the level of output (e.g. raw materials, hourly-paid labour).
Total Cost (TC)
The sum of fixed and variable costs at a given level of output (TC = FC + VC).
Law of Diminishing Returns
In the short run, as more of a variable factor (e.g. labour) is added to a fixed factor (e.g. capital), the marginal product of the variable factor will eventually fall — directly causing short-run marginal cost to eventually rise.
Fig 2: Fixed, variable and total cost curves. FC is constant regardless of output (a horizontal line); VC rises with output; TC is the vertical sum of the two at every output level.
Why Marginal Cost Eventually Rises (The Law of Diminishing Returns): in the short run, as a firm adds more workers to a fixed amount of capital and space, each additional worker eventually adds less extra output than the worker before them (diminishing marginal returns) — meaning each additional unit of output costs progressively more to produce, which is precisely why short-run marginal and average cost curves are U-shaped rather than constant or ever-falling.

Long-Run Costs

In the long run, a firm can vary all factors of production, including the scale of its capital — allowing it to choose the most cost-efficient scale of operation rather than being constrained by a fixed factor.

Fig 3: The long-run average cost (LRAC) curve as the "envelope" of many short-run average cost (SRAC) curves, each representing a different fixed scale of plant. As a firm grows, it moves along the LRAC by switching to progressively larger SRAC curves, each with a lower minimum point — until diseconomies of scale (Section 9) cause the LRAC to turn upward again.
3.3.3

Economies and Diseconomies of Scale

As a firm increases its scale of production in the long run, average costs typically fall at first (economies of scale) before eventually rising again (diseconomies of scale) — directly shaping the LRAC curve introduced in Fig. 3.

Economies of Scale
Falling long-run average cost as output increases, arising from the benefits of large-scale production.
Diseconomies of Scale
Rising long-run average cost as output increases beyond a certain point, arising from the difficulties of managing a very large-scale operation.
Minimum Efficient Scale (MES)
The lowest level of output at which a firm can achieve the minimum possible long-run average cost — producing below this scale means missing out on some available economies of scale.
Type of Internal Economy of ScaleExplanation
Purchasing (bulk-buying) economiesLarge firms can negotiate discounts from suppliers by buying raw materials in bulk.
Technical economiesLarge-scale production allows the use of specialised, more efficient machinery and greater division of labour.
Financial economiesLarge firms typically access finance at lower interest rates, being seen as lower risk by lenders.
Managerial economiesLarge firms can afford to employ specialist managers (finance, marketing, HR) rather than relying on generalists.
Risk-bearing economiesLarge, diversified firms can spread risk across multiple products or markets.
Type of Diseconomy of ScaleExplanation
Communication difficultiesInformation can be delayed, lost, or distorted as it passes through a larger organisational hierarchy.
Coordination difficultiesCoordinating many departments, sites, and staff becomes progressively harder as a firm grows.
Motivational/alienation issuesWorkers in a very large organisation can feel disconnected from decision-making, reducing morale and productivity.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Supermarket Bulk-Buying Power
Large UK supermarket chains can negotiate significantly lower per-unit prices from suppliers than small independent grocers, due to the sheer scale of their orders (purchasing economies) — a major source of the price advantage large supermarkets hold over smaller competitors, and a direct illustration of why minimum efficient scale in grocery retail tends to be very large.
External Economies of Scale — A Distinct Concept: economies of scale can also arise outside an individual firm, from the growth of an entire industry in a particular location — for example, the concentration of financial services firms in the City of London allows every firm located there to benefit from a shared pool of specialist skilled labour, supporting infrastructure, and reputation, regardless of any individual firm's own internal scale.
3.3.4

Normal Profit, Supernormal Profit and Losses

Economists define profit somewhat differently from everyday usage, distinguishing between the minimum profit needed to keep a firm in business and any additional profit earned above that minimum.

Normal Profit
The minimum level of profit required to keep a firm's owner supplying their enterprise to this particular market, equal to the opportunity cost of the resources they have invested — economically, normal profit is treated as a cost of production, not a surplus.
Supernormal (Economic) Profit
Any profit earned above normal profit — a genuine economic surplus, occurring where AR exceeds AC.
Loss
A situation where AR is less than AC — the firm is not even covering its full costs, including normal profit.
Fig 4: Total revenue and total cost. Where the TR line lies above the TC line, the firm earns profit; where TC lies above TR, the firm makes a loss; the two points where the lines cross are the break-even points.

Worked Example — Normal vs Supernormal Profit

A firm's opportunity cost of capital invested (normal profit) is £50,000 per year. Its accounting profit (revenue minus explicit costs) this year is £70,000.

Supernormal (economic) profit = Accounting profit − Normal profit = £70,000 − £50,000 = £20,000

Key point: the firm is earning £20,000 of genuine economic surplus above the minimum needed to keep it operating in this market — this is the "supernormal profit" market structure models (Section 3.4) refer to.

SituationRelationshipLong-Run Implication
Supernormal profitAR > ACAttracts new entrants if barriers to entry are low (Section 12), competing away the supernormal profit over time.
Normal profitAR = ACFirms have no incentive to enter or exit — a stable long-run equilibrium.
Loss (subnormal profit)AR < ACFirms exit the market over time if losses persist and barriers to exit are low.
Why This Distinction Is Central to Market Structure Theory: the entire logic of how perfect competition (Section 12) erodes supernormal profit to zero in the long run, while monopoly (Section 16) can sustain supernormal profit indefinitely, rests directly on this normal/supernormal profit distinction combined with the presence or absence of barriers to entry — make sure this distinction is completely secure before moving into Section 3.4's market structure models.
3.4.1

Economic Efficiency

Before examining individual market structures, it is essential to define the different types of efficiency economists use to judge how well a market structure serves society — each market structure examined in this section will be assessed against these benchmarks.

Productive Efficiency
Occurs where a firm produces at the lowest point on its average cost curve, using the minimum possible resources to produce a given output.
Allocative Efficiency
Occurs where price equals marginal cost (P=MC) — resources are allocated exactly according to consumers' willingness to pay relative to the true cost of production, maximising total welfare.
Dynamic Efficiency
Occurs where a firm invests in innovation, research and development over time, improving products and production processes — a long-run, dynamic form of efficiency rather than a snapshot at one point in time.
X-Inefficiency
A situation where a firm's actual costs are higher than the minimum technically achievable cost, typically because a lack of competitive pressure removes the incentive to control costs tightly.
Type of EfficiencyConditionMarket Structure Most Associated With It
Productive efficiencyProducing at minimum ACPerfect competition in the long run (Section 12)
Allocative efficiencyP = MCPerfect competition (Section 12)
Dynamic efficiencyOngoing investment in innovationOligopoly and monopoly, where supernormal profit can fund R&D (Sections 14, 16)
X-inefficiencyActual cost > minimum possible costMonopoly, where weak competitive pressure can allow costs to drift upward (Section 16)
Why Allocative and Productive Efficiency Can Conflict with Dynamic Efficiency: perfect competition achieves allocative and productive efficiency in the standard static model, but the resulting absence of supernormal profit (Section 10) leaves firms with little surplus to invest in research and development — while a monopolist, though allocatively and productively inefficient in the static sense, may generate substantial dynamic efficiency by using supernormal profit to fund innovation. This trade-off between static and dynamic efficiency is one of the most important evaluative themes running through the whole of Section 3.4.
3.4.2

Perfect Competition

Perfect competition is a theoretical market structure built on a set of idealised assumptions, providing the benchmark against which every other, more realistic market structure in this section is compared.

Perfect Competition
A market structure characterised by: many buyers and sellers, none large enough to influence the market price; a homogeneous (identical) product; perfect information; freedom of entry and exit; and firms as "price takers," accepting the market price rather than setting it.
Fig 5: In perfect competition, the market (left) determines price through overall demand and supply, and the individual firm (right) takes this price as given — its own demand curve is perfectly elastic (horizontal) at the market price, since it has no power to influence price by varying its own output.
Fig 6: A perfectly competitive firm earning supernormal profit in the short run, where AR (=price) exceeds AC at the profit-maximising output (MC=MR).
1
Supernormal profit attracts new entrants

Because entry is free (a defining assumption of perfect competition), the supernormal profit shown in Fig. 6 signals an opportunity that new firms will move to exploit.

2
New entrants increase market supply, driving down market price

As more firms enter, total market output rises, shifting market supply right and reducing the market price every existing firm must accept.

3
Entry continues until supernormal profit is competed away entirely

Firms keep entering until price falls to the point where AR = AC exactly — normal profit only, removing any further incentive to enter.

Fig 7: Long-run equilibrium in perfect competition. AR=AC at the profit-maximising output — only normal profit is earned, removing any further incentive for firms to enter or exit. Note this occurs exactly at the minimum point of the AC curve, meaning perfectly competitive firms are productively efficient in long-run equilibrium, and since P=MC, they are allocatively efficient too (Section 11).
Why Perfect Competition Is a Benchmark, Not a Description of Reality: genuinely perfect competition rarely, if ever, exists exactly as defined — but its value lies precisely in providing a theoretical benchmark of maximum static efficiency (Section 11) against which the real-world market structures in Sections 13–19 can be judged and compared.
3.4.3

Monopolistic Competition

Monopolistic competition relaxes one key assumption of perfect competition — the identical, homogeneous product — bringing the model considerably closer to many real-world markets.

Monopolistic Competition
A market structure with many firms, freedom of entry and exit, but a differentiated product — each firm has some limited market power due to branding, quality, or location differences, giving it a downward-sloping (rather than horizontal) demand curve.
Product Differentiation
Making a firm's product distinct from competitors' through branding, quality, features, or customer service, allowing a firm some independent influence over price.
Fig 8: Long-run equilibrium in monopolistic competition. Just as in perfect competition, free entry competes away supernormal profit until only normal profit remains (AR=AC) — but because the firm's demand curve is downward-sloping rather than horizontal, this occurs to the left of the minimum point of the AC curve, meaning the firm is not fully productively efficient, unlike a perfectly competitive firm in Fig. 7.
FeaturePerfect CompetitionMonopolistic Competition
ProductHomogeneous (identical)Differentiated (branded, distinct)
Demand curve facing the firmPerfectly elastic (horizontal)Downward-sloping, but relatively elastic due to many close substitutes
Long-run profitNormal profit onlyNormal profit only
Productive efficiency in long runYes (at minimum AC)No (slightly above minimum AC)
★ UK EXAMPLE: Independent Coffee Shops and Hairdressers
Local coffee shops, hairdressers, and restaurants typically operate in monopolistically competitive markets — many small firms compete for local custom, entry and exit are relatively easy, but each business differentiates itself through branding, location, décor, and personal service, giving it some limited pricing power that a perfectly competitive firm would entirely lack.
Non-Price Competition: because monopolistically competitive firms have some independent pricing power, they frequently compete on factors other than price — branding, advertising, product quality, and customer service — a form of competition that is largely absent from the perfect competition model, where an identical product leaves no scope for differentiation at all.
3.4.4

Oligopoly

An oligopoly is a market dominated by a small number of large firms, whose decisions are directly and visibly interdependent — a fundamentally different competitive dynamic from the many small, independent firms assumed in Sections 12–13.

Oligopoly
A market structure dominated by a small number of large firms, each with significant market power, whose pricing and output decisions are interdependent — each firm must consider how rivals will react to its own decisions.
Interdependence
The defining feature of oligopoly: because a small number of firms account for most industry output, any one firm's decisions (on price, output, or advertising) directly and predictably affect its rivals, who are likely to respond.
Concentration Ratio
The combined market share of the largest firms in an industry (e.g. a 4-firm concentration ratio, CR4, measures the combined share of the four largest firms) — a common way of identifying and measuring oligopoly.
Fig 9: The kinked demand curve model of oligopoly. Above the current price, demand is relatively elastic (rivals do not follow a price rise, so the firm loses substantial market share by raising price); below the current price, demand is relatively inelastic (rivals match a price cut to avoid losing share, so little extra volume is gained). This asymmetry creates a kink in the demand curve at the current price, and a discontinuous "gap" in the MR curve.
Fig 10: Price stability under the kinked demand model. Because the MR curve has a vertical discontinuity at the kink, marginal cost can shift up or down substantially (from MC1 to MC2) without crossing MR at a different quantity — meaning the profit-maximising price and output remain unchanged even as costs change, helping explain why oligopoly prices are often observed to be relatively stable (or "sticky") compared with other market structures.
Type of Oligopoly BehaviourExplanation
Collusion (cartels)Firms explicitly or tacitly agree to restrict output or fix prices, behaving collectively like a monopoly to raise joint profit — illegal under UK and EU competition law when explicit (Section 25).
Price warsAggressive price-cutting between rival firms, sometimes to force weaker competitors out of the market.
Non-price competitionCompeting through advertising, loyalty schemes, product innovation, or service quality rather than price, avoiding the risk of a mutually damaging price war.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The UK Grocery Retail Oligopoly
UK grocery retail is dominated by a small number of major supermarket chains, which together hold a high combined concentration ratio. Price changes by one major chain are typically matched quickly by rivals (interdependence in action), while much competitive activity takes place through loyalty card schemes, own-brand ranges, and store experience — non-price competition — rather than sustained price wars, which would damage industry-wide profitability.
Why the Kinked Demand Model Has Limitations: despite its intuitive appeal, the kinked demand model does not explain how the initial price was determined in the first place, only why it might remain stable once established — a commonly tested evaluative point when discussing this model's usefulness.
3.4.4

Game Theory and the Prisoner's Dilemma

Game theory provides a formal way of analysing the strategic interdependence between oligopolists introduced in Section 14, modelling how each firm's best decision depends on what it expects its rivals to do.

Game Theory
The study of strategic decision-making where the outcome for each participant depends not only on their own choice, but on the choices made by others.
Payoff Matrix
A table showing the outcome (payoff) for each possible combination of strategies chosen by two or more players.
Dominant Strategy
A strategy that gives a player the best outcome regardless of what the other player chooses to do.
Nash Equilibrium
A stable outcome in which no player can improve their own payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy, given the strategy chosen by the other player.
Firm B: Keep Price HighFirm B: Cut Price
Firm A: Keep Price HighA: £100m, B: £100mA: £40m, B: £120m
Firm A: Cut PriceA: £120m, B: £40mA: £70m, B: £70m

Worked Example — Solving the Prisoner's Dilemma

Using the payoff matrix above: if Firm B keeps its price high, Firm A earns more by cutting price (£120m vs £100m). If Firm B cuts its price, Firm A still earns more by cutting price (£70m vs £40m). Cutting price is therefore Firm A's dominant strategy — the best choice regardless of what Firm B does. By symmetry, cutting price is also Firm B's dominant strategy.

Nash equilibrium: both firms cut price, each earning £70m
Joint (collusive) optimum: both firms keep price high, each earning £100m

Key point: both firms would be individually better off colluding to keep prices high (£100m each) than they are in the non-cooperative Nash equilibrium (£70m each) — yet rational self-interest, absent trust or enforcement, drives both firms to the worse, mutually damaging outcome. This is the essence of the "prisoner's dilemma."

Why This Model Explains Both Collusion and Its Fragility: the prisoner's dilemma shows exactly why oligopolists have a strong incentive to collude (Section 14) — achieving the higher joint payoff — but also why collusion is inherently unstable without external enforcement: each firm always has an individual incentive to secretly break the agreement and undercut its rival, exactly as cutting price remains individually rational even when the other firm is cooperating. Repeated interaction between the same firms over time (rather than a single one-off game) can support more stable cooperation, since a firm that cheats today can expect retaliation in future rounds.
3.4.5

Monopoly

At the opposite extreme from perfect competition, a monopoly exists where a single firm dominates a market, facing no direct competition and holding substantial control over price.

Monopoly
A market structure in which a single firm supplies the entire (or an overwhelmingly dominant share of the) market, protected by significant barriers to entry.
Barriers to Entry
Obstacles that prevent new firms from entering a market, allowing incumbent firms to sustain supernormal profit in the long run — including economies of scale (Section 9), legal protections (patents, licences), and control of essential resources.
Fig 11: Profit maximisation under monopoly. The firm sets output where MC=MR, then charges the price read off the demand (AR) curve at that output — since AR exceeds AC at this output, the firm earns supernormal profit. Unlike perfect competition, high barriers to entry mean this supernormal profit is not competed away in the long run.
Assessing MonopolyArgument
Against monopolyOutput is restricted and price raised above the competitive level, causing allocative inefficiency (P>MC, Section 11) and a deadweight loss of consumer welfare; weak competitive pressure can also lead to X-inefficiency (Section 11).
For monopolySustained supernormal profit can fund research and development, supporting dynamic efficiency (Section 11); monopolies may also be natural in industries with very high fixed costs, where a single large firm can achieve economies of scale a fragmented competitive market could not.
Natural Monopoly: in industries with extremely high fixed infrastructure costs relative to demand (e.g. water supply, rail networks), a single firm can supply the entire market at a lower average cost than if the market were divided between several competing firms each duplicating the same infrastructure — a "natural monopoly," where monopoly may actually be the most efficient market structure, provided its pricing is regulated (Section 27) to prevent abuse of its market power.
3.4.5

Price Discrimination

Firms with market power, such as monopolies, can sometimes increase their profit further by charging different prices to different consumers for an identical product — a practice known as price discrimination.

Price Discrimination
Charging different prices to different consumers for the same good or service, where the price difference is not justified by any difference in the cost of supply.
Necessary ConditionExplanation
Market powerThe firm must be able to influence price, ruling out perfectly competitive firms, which are price takers (Section 12).
Ability to separate marketsThe firm must be able to identify and separate consumer groups with different price sensitivities.
Prevention of resaleConsumers who buy at the lower price must be unable to resell to those facing the higher price, or the price discrimination collapses.
Fig 12: Third-degree price discrimination. The firm splits the market into sub-markets by elasticity of demand, charging a higher price in the market with more inelastic demand (less price-sensitive consumers) and a lower price where demand is more elastic — maximising total revenue and profit relative to a single uniform price.
Fig 13: First-degree (perfect) price discrimination, where a firm charges each individual consumer exactly their own maximum willingness to pay. Compared with single-price monopoly, this converts all consumer surplus into producer surplus, and — notably — restores allocative efficiency by expanding output to the point where price equals marginal cost, since the firm no longer needs to lower the price for all units to sell one more.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Rail Fares and Peak/Off-Peak Pricing
UK train operators charge substantially higher fares during peak commuting hours than for identical journeys off-peak — a clear case of third-degree price discrimination, splitting the market by time of travel into a relatively inelastic peak segment (commuters with few alternatives) and a more elastic off-peak segment (leisure travellers more willing to shift their travel time or use alternatives).

Case For Price Discrimination

  • Can expand output and improve accessibility for price-sensitive groups (e.g. student and pensioner discounts), who might otherwise be priced out entirely.
  • First-degree discrimination can improve allocative efficiency by expanding output towards the competitive level (Fig. 13).
  • Additional revenue can help fund high fixed costs, supporting service provision that might not otherwise be commercially viable.

Case Against Price Discrimination

  • Transfers consumer surplus to the firm, generally worsening the distribution of welfare between firms and consumers.
  • Can be viewed as unfair if based on characteristics unrelated to willingness to pay.
  • Requires market power to sustain, so profits gained through price discrimination reflect and reinforce that market power (Section 16).
3.4.6

Monopsony

A monopsony is the mirror image of a monopoly: rather than a single dominant seller, a monopsony is a single dominant buyer in a market — most commonly examined in Theme 3 in the context of a dominant employer in a local labour market.

Monopsony
A market with a single, or dominant, buyer, giving that buyer significant power to influence the price (or wage) it pays, in contrast to a price-taking buyer in a competitive market.
Fig 14: A monopsony employer. Because the firm is the only significant buyer of labour, it faces the entire upward-sloping market labour supply curve (S=AC) — to hire one more worker, it must raise the wage for all workers, not just the marginal hire, making its marginal cost of labour (MC) lie above the average cost (S=AC) curve. The firm hires where MC=D (=MRP), then pays only the wage read off the supply curve at that quantity — a wage below the competitive level, and an employment level also below the competitive level.
StakeholderEffect of Monopsony Power
WorkersReceive a lower wage and face lower employment than in a competitive labour market — a clear cost.
The employerBenefits from lower wage costs, potentially allowing lower prices for consumers or higher profit for reinvestment.
ConsumersMay benefit from lower prices if the employer's cost savings are passed on, though this is not guaranteed.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The NHS as a Monopsony Employer
The NHS is the dominant employer of nurses and many other healthcare professionals across much of the UK, giving it substantial monopsony power in this specific labour market — a significant reason trade unions (Section 23) play an important role in NHS pay negotiations, since individual workers have very limited bargaining power against a single, dominant buyer of their labour.
Why Monopsony Connects Directly to Section 23: the wage and employment outcomes under monopsony examined here form the essential foundation for understanding how trade unions and the National Minimum Wage can, under specific conditions, actually raise both wages and employment simultaneously in a monopsony labour market — a counterintuitive result explored fully in Sections 23 and 26.
3.4.7

Contestable Markets

The theory of contestable markets argues that the number of firms currently operating in a market matters less than how easily new firms could enter it — a market can behave competitively even with very few incumbent firms, provided entry and exit are easy.

Contestable Market
A market with low barriers to entry and exit, particularly low (or zero) sunk costs, meaning potential competition from new entrants disciplines incumbent firms' behaviour even if few firms currently operate in the market.
Sunk Costs
Costs that, once incurred, cannot be recovered upon leaving a market (e.g. specialised advertising or equipment with no resale value) — low sunk costs are the single most important condition for a market to be contestable, since they allow "hit and run" entry with minimal risk.
Hit-and-Run Entry
A new firm enters a contestable market to capture short-run supernormal profit, then exits quickly (at low cost, due to low sunk costs) if incumbents respond by cutting price.
Fig 15: A non-contestable monopoly earning sustained supernormal profit (left) compared with a contestable market (right), where the threat of hit-and-run entry forces the incumbent to set price close to average cost — limiting profit to close to normal levels, even though only one or few firms currently operate in the market.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Budget Airline Routes
Specific airline routes can behave as highly contestable markets: aircraft (the main capital investment) can be redeployed to different routes relatively easily if a route becomes unprofitable, meaning sunk costs specific to any one route are low. This allows budget airlines to enter and exit specific routes quickly in response to profit opportunities, disciplining incumbent pricing on that route even where only one or two airlines currently operate it.
Why Contestability Theory Challenges the Traditional Market Structure Spectrum: traditional market structure analysis (Sections 12–16) predicts firm behaviour primarily from the number of firms currently in a market — contestability theory instead argues that the threat of entry can be just as powerful a competitive discipline as actual current competition, meaning a market with very few firms (structurally resembling an oligopoly or monopoly) can still produce close to competitive outcomes if barriers to entry and exit, especially sunk costs, are low.
3.5.1

Demand for Labour

The demand for labour is a derived demand — firms do not want labour for its own sake, but because of what labour helps produce. This single idea underpins almost everything else in the labour market topic.

Derived Demand
Demand for a factor of production (such as labour) that exists only because of the demand for the final good or service that factor helps to produce.
Marginal Revenue Product of Labour (MRP)
The additional revenue a firm gains from employing one more worker, calculated as the extra output that worker produces (marginal physical product) multiplied by the price at which that output is sold (MRP = MPP × Price).

Worked Example — Calculating MRP

A worker's marginal physical product is 20 units of output per week. Each unit sells for £15.

MRP = MPP × Price = 20 × £15 = £300 per week

Key point: a profit-maximising firm will only continue to employ additional workers while their MRP exceeds the wage they must be paid — the firm's demand curve for labour is, in fact, its MRP curve.

Determinant of Labour DemandEffect of an Increase
Demand for the final good/serviceHigher product demand raises MRP (more output can be sold at the given price), increasing labour demand.
Labour productivityHigher productivity raises MPP and therefore MRP, increasing labour demand.
Price of the final good/serviceA higher output price raises MRP directly, increasing labour demand.
Price of capital (substitute factor)More expensive capital can increase demand for labour as a relatively cheaper substitute factor.
Fig 16: Skill-biased technological change. Automation reduces MRP (and therefore demand) for low-skilled labour whose tasks can be automated, while increasing MRP (and demand) for high-skilled labour that operates, maintains, or complements the new technology — a major driver of widening wage inequality between skill groups.

Elasticity of Demand for Labour

DeterminantTends Towards Inelastic DemandTends Towards Elastic Demand
Labour cost as a share of total costSmall shareLarge share
Ease of substituting capital for labourDifficult to substituteEasy to substitute
Elasticity of demand for the final productInelastic product demandElastic product demand
Time periodShort runLong run
Fig 17: Elastic vs inelastic labour demand. A given shift in labour supply (or a wage change) produces a much larger change in employment where labour demand is elastic, compared with a market where labour demand is inelastic — directly relevant to predicting the employment impact of policies such as the National Minimum Wage (Section 26).
3.5.2

Supply of Labour and Labour Market Immobility

The supply of labour reflects individuals' willingness and ability to work, and is shaped by both monetary and non-monetary factors — but even where workers are willing to supply their labour, immobility can prevent them reaching the jobs where they are most needed.

Supply of Labour
The quantity of labour that workers are willing and able to supply at a given wage rate, in a given occupation, industry, or economy.
Determinant of Labour SupplyExplanation
Wage rateHigher wages typically increase quantity of labour supplied, though the backward-bending supply curve (below) shows this can reverse at very high wages.
Non-monetary factorsJob satisfaction, working conditions, holiday entitlement, and status all influence willingness to supply labour to a specific occupation, independent of wage.
Population and net migrationA larger working-age population, including via net inward migration, increases the total supply of labour to the economy.
Barriers to entry to an occupationRequired qualifications, licensing, or training length can restrict labour supply to specific skilled occupations.
Fig 18: The backward-bending individual labour supply curve. At relatively low wages, a wage rise increases hours supplied (the substitution effect of a higher reward for work dominates). Beyond a certain wage, a further wage rise can actually reduce hours supplied, as workers choose to "buy" more leisure time with their now-higher income (the income effect dominates) — an individual-level phenomenon that does not necessarily apply to the whole-market labour supply curve.

Labour Market Immobility

Geographical Immobility
Difficulty moving labour from one geographic area to another, due to barriers such as relocation cost, family and social ties, or regional differences in housing cost.
Occupational Immobility
Difficulty moving labour from one occupation to another, due to a lack of transferable skills or the time and cost of retraining.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Former Coalfield Communities
Many former coal mining communities in South Wales, Yorkshire, and the North East of England experienced persistently elevated unemployment for decades following pit closures in the 1980s and 1990s, even as vacancies existed in service-sector and technology roles elsewhere in the UK. Occupational immobility (mining-specific skills not transferring easily to office or digital roles) and geographical immobility (the cost and disruption of relocating, alongside strong community ties) both contributed to labour remaining unemployed locally rather than moving to where it was in demand.
Why Immobility Is a Form of Labour Market Failure: immobility means labour, a scarce resource, can sit unemployed in one part of the economy while unfilled vacancies persist elsewhere — a clear misallocation of resources that the wage mechanism alone has failed to correct, and a key justification for the government policies examined in Section 26.
3.5.3

Wage Determination in Competitive Markets

In a perfectly competitive labour market, the wage rate is determined in exactly the same way as price in any competitive product market — by the intersection of market demand and market supply — with each individual firm and worker acting as a wage taker.

Perfectly Competitive Labour Market
A labour market with many buyers (firms) and many sellers (workers) of a homogeneous type of labour, perfect information, and no single firm or worker large enough to influence the wage rate.
Wage Taker
A firm or worker that must accept the market-determined wage rate, having no individual power to influence it — directly analogous to a price-taking firm in perfect competition (Section 12).
1
Market labour demand (the sum of all firms' MRP curves) and market labour supply determine the equilibrium wage

Exactly as with any competitive market, the intersection of demand and supply sets the market-clearing wage rate.

2
Every individual firm takes this wage as given

A single firm employing a small share of the total market workforce cannot influence the wage by hiring more or fewer workers — it faces a perfectly elastic (horizontal) labour supply curve at the market wage.

3
Each firm hires up to the point where MRP equals the market wage

Since the firm can hire as many workers as it wants at the going wage, it maximises profit by hiring exactly up to the point where the additional revenue from the next worker (MRP) equals their cost (the wage).

Why This Model Is a Benchmark, Not a Description of Every Labour Market: exactly as with perfect competition in product markets, a perfectly competitive labour market is a theoretical benchmark rather than a common real-world description — most real labour markets display at least some non-competitive features (monopsony power, trade unions, government wage regulation), examined in Section 23, making the comparison between the two essential for exam evaluation.
3.5.3

Wage Determination in Non-Competitive Markets

Most real-world labour markets depart from the competitive benchmark in Section 22 in at least one direction — through monopsony employer power, or through trade unions acting on behalf of workers.

Fig 19: Monopsony wage determination (see also Fig. 14, Section 18). A monopsony employer pays a wage below, and employs fewer workers than, the competitive equilibrium — the wage and employment gap is a direct measure of the employer's monopsony power.
Trade Union
An organisation of workers that negotiates collectively with employers over pay and working conditions, using collective bargaining power that individual workers acting alone would lack.
Collective Bargaining
Wage negotiation conducted by a trade union on behalf of its members as a group, rather than each worker negotiating individually.
Fig 20: A trade union acting as a counterweight to monopsony power. By negotiating a wage above the monopsony wage (up to the competitive wage), a trade union can raise both the wage and the level of employment simultaneously — a genuinely counterintuitive result that only holds in a monopsony labour market; in a competitive labour market, a trade union pushing wages above the equilibrium would instead cause unemployment, exactly as a minimum wage would (Section 26).
Factor Affecting Trade Union Bargaining PowerExplanation
Union densityThe proportion of the workforce belonging to a union — higher density strengthens collective bargaining power.
Elasticity of demand for labourWhere labour demand is inelastic (Section 20), unions can negotiate higher wages with a smaller employment cost.
Ability to take industrial actionThe credible threat of strike action strengthens a union's negotiating position, particularly in essential services with few substitutes.
Legal frameworkUK legislation since the 1980s has placed significant restrictions on industrial action, affecting union bargaining power.
★ UK EXAMPLE: RMT and Rail Sector Strikes
The RMT (National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers) has historically demonstrated significant bargaining power in pay negotiations with UK rail operators, partly reflecting the difficulty of substituting striking rail workers at short notice and the high public visibility (and disruption cost) of rail strikes — illustrating how the specific factors in the table above translate into real negotiating outcomes.
Exam Application: when analysing a labour market question, always establish first whether the market described is closer to the competitive benchmark (Section 22) or displays monopsony and/or trade union characteristics (this section) — the correct diagram, and the direction of any policy's predicted effect, depends entirely on this initial classification.
3.5.3

Wage Differentials

Wage differentials — persistent pay gaps between different occupations, industries, or demographic groups — can be explained largely by applying the demand and supply framework from Sections 20–21 to specific labour markets.

Fig 21: Wage differentials explained by demand and supply. Neurosurgeons combine very high demand (high MRP, given the scarce and highly specialised skill involved) with severely restricted supply (many years of rigorous training and selection) — producing a very high equilibrium wage. Cleaners face relatively lower demand and a much larger potential supply of workers able to do the job with little training, producing a much lower equilibrium wage.
Cause of Wage DifferentialsExplanation
Differences in marginal revenue productOccupations where workers generate more revenue per hour (Section 20) command higher wages, all else equal.
Differences in labour supply (skill/training barriers)Occupations requiring rare skills or lengthy training have restricted supply, raising equilibrium wages.
Compensating wage differentialsUnpleasant, dangerous, or unsociable jobs may need to offer a wage premium to attract sufficient workers, compensating for the non-monetary disadvantages of the role.
Monopsony and trade union powerThe presence or absence of employer monopsony power and union bargaining strength (Section 23) directly affects the wage an occupation or industry can sustain.
DiscriminationWage differences between groups performing equivalent work, not explained by productivity differences, can reflect labour market discrimination rather than any of the factors above.
Occupational Crowding as a Cause of Discriminatory Wage Differentials: where discrimination or social barriers restrict a group's access to higher-paid occupations, that group can become "crowded" into a narrower range of lower-paid occupations — artificially increasing labour supply (and depressing wages) in those crowded occupations, while artificially restricting supply (and inflating wages) in the occupations from which the group is effectively excluded. This remains one of the most important economic explanations for persistent gender and ethnicity pay gaps, distinct from differences in individual productivity.
3.6.1

Government Intervention to Promote Competition

Given the market power examined throughout Section 3.4, governments maintain a range of tools specifically aimed at controlling monopoly power, promoting competition, and reviewing mergers.

InterventionAimUK Example
Merger controlPrevent mergers that would substantially reduce competition (Section 2), or approve them only subject to conditions.The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) investigates and can block or condition significant UK mergers.
Price regulationCap the price a monopoly (particularly a natural monopoly, Section 16) can charge, preventing exploitation of market power.Ofwat, Ofgem, and Ofcom each cap prices in their respective privatised utility sectors.
Profit regulationLimit the rate of return a regulated firm can earn on its capital, indirectly constraining prices.Historically used alongside price-cap regulation in some UK utility sectors.
Performance targets and quality standardsRequire regulated firms to meet minimum service quality, protecting consumers where price competition alone is insufficient.Rail performance targets set by the Office of Rail and Road (ORR).
Promoting small business growthLower barriers to entry and support contestability (Section 19) by helping smaller firms compete.UK government grants, business rates relief, and start-up loan schemes for small and medium enterprises.
Deregulation and privatisationRemove state monopoly and open markets to competition.Privatisation of British Telecom, British Gas, and the water and electricity industries from the 1980s.
Competitive tenderingGovernment contracts are auctioned competitively rather than awarded automatically to an incumbent, encouraging efficiency even in public-sector-adjacent provision.Competitive tendering for NHS support services and local authority contracts.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)
The CMA is the UK's principal competition regulator, responsible for investigating mergers, cartels, and abuses of market power, and for market studies into sectors of particular concern such as UK grocery retail and digital markets. Where the CMA finds a merger would substantially reduce competition, it can require the merging firms to sell off parts of the combined business, or block the merger outright.
Why Different Market Structures Need Different Interventions: the appropriate intervention depends heavily on the underlying market structure (Section 3.4) — merger control is most relevant where horizontal integration (Section 2) threatens to create excessive concentration; price/profit regulation is most relevant for natural monopolies (Section 16) where competition itself is not feasible; and promoting contestability (Section 19) is most relevant where genuine competition between multiple firms is achievable but currently weak.
3.6.1

Government Intervention in Labour Markets

Government also intervenes directly in the labour market, most visibly through minimum and maximum wage legislation, but also through public sector wage-setting and policies targeting labour market immobility (Section 21).

National Minimum Wage / National Living Wage
A legally binding minimum hourly wage rate that employers must pay, set above the free-market equilibrium wage in the specific labour markets it is intended to affect.
Maximum Wage
A legal cap on the wage that can be paid for a specific role, occasionally proposed (for example, capping executive pay) to address concerns about excessive top-end pay, though far less commonly implemented in practice than minimum wage legislation.
Fig 22: A minimum wage set above the competitive equilibrium wage in a competitive labour market. Quantity of labour supplied rises while quantity demanded falls, creating excess supply of labour — real wage (classical) unemployment, shown by the shaded triangle.
Fig 23: A minimum wage in a monopsony labour market (contrast with Fig. 22). Set correctly (up to the competitive wage), a minimum wage can raise both the wage and employment simultaneously in a monopsony market, exactly as a trade union can (Section 23, Fig. 20) — but if set above the competitive wage, unemployment results exactly as in the competitive case.
Why the Employment Effect of a Minimum Wage Depends Entirely on Market Structure: this is one of the most heavily tested applications in the whole labour market topic — the same policy (a minimum wage) causes unemployment in a competitive labour market (Fig. 22) but can raise employment in a monopsony labour market (Fig. 23), provided it is not set above the competitive wage level. Always identify the underlying market structure before predicting the employment effect of a wage floor.
Further Labour Market InterventionExplanation
Public sector wage settingGovernment directly sets pay for public sector workers (e.g. via independent pay review bodies for NHS staff and teachers), balancing recruitment/retention needs against the fiscal cost to taxpayers.
Policies to tackle labour market immobilityRetraining schemes, apprenticeships, and relocation support aim to directly address the occupational and geographical immobility examined in Section 21.
Legislation on trade unionsUK law regulates the circumstances under which industrial action can lawfully take place, directly affecting union bargaining power (Section 23).
3.6.2

The Impact and Limits of Government Intervention

Government intervention in both product and labour markets carries real benefits, but also real costs and limitations — exam answers should weigh both explicitly rather than assuming intervention automatically improves outcomes.

Likely Benefits of Intervention

  • Lower consumer prices where monopoly power is successfully constrained (Section 25).
  • Reduced supernormal profit for dominant firms, redistributing surplus towards consumers.
  • Improved product/service quality and efficiency, where regulation creates pressure that weak competition alone did not.
  • Increased consumer choice, where merger control or contestability policy successfully preserves competition.
  • Higher wages and/or employment in monopsony labour markets, where minimum wage or union bargaining power is correctly calibrated (Section 26).

Likely Costs and Limits of Intervention

  • Asymmetric information: regulators may lack the detailed information about a firm's true costs needed to set an optimal price cap or performance target, risking an incorrectly calibrated intervention.
  • Regulatory capture: a regulator can become too closely aligned with the interests of the firms it regulates, weakening the intervention's effectiveness.
  • Administrative and compliance costs: designing, monitoring, and enforcing regulation consumes real resources for both the regulator and regulated firms.
  • Risk of unintended consequences: a minimum wage set too high in a competitive labour market, for example, causes the unemployment shown in Fig. 22 rather than the intended benefit.
  • Reduced investment incentives: capping profit or price too aggressively can reduce a regulated firm's incentive or ability to invest in long-run improvements, undermining dynamic efficiency (Section 11).
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Regulatory Capture in Financial Regulation
UK and international financial regulators in the years preceding the 2008 financial crisis were widely criticised, in retrospect, for having become too closely aligned with the banks they supervised — a light-touch regulatory approach was in part justified by regulators' close working relationships with, and reliance on information provided by, the banking industry itself, an example of regulatory capture limiting the effectiveness of intervention that was nominally in place.
Exam Application: the strongest evaluative answers on government intervention always conclude with an explicit, reasoned judgement about whether the specific benefit outweighs the specific cost or limitation in the given context — rather than treating intervention as automatically beneficial simply because a market failure or market power problem has been correctly identified.
Exam Prep

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Barriers to Entry to Government Intervention

A firm holds significant market power due to high barriers to entry (Section 16) → it sets output where MC=MR and charges the price read off its demand curve, earning sustained supernormal profit → because P>MC, this outcome is allocatively inefficient, and weak competitive pressure may also permit X-inefficiency (Section 11) → this combination of excess profit and potential inefficiency provides the case for government intervention (Section 25) → government imposes price-cap regulation, requiring the firm to charge closer to average cost → if the cap is set too low, the firm's incentive to invest in quality or innovation may fall, illustrating the dynamic-efficiency cost that must be weighed against the static efficiency gain (Section 27).

The Golden Chain: From Monopsony Power to a Corrective Minimum Wage

A single employer dominates a local labour market (Section 18) → it faces the entire upward-sloping labour supply curve, giving its marginal cost of labour curve a steeper slope than the supply curve itself → it maximises profit where MC=MRP, then pays only the wage read off the supply curve at that quantity — a wage, and employment level, below the competitive outcome → workers have little individual bargaining power to correct this, since there is no alternative employer to switch to → a trade union (Section 23) or a correctly calibrated minimum wage (Section 26) can raise the wage up to the competitive level, simultaneously increasing employment as well as pay — but only up to that competitive wage; setting either tool higher than the competitive wage would cause unemployment exactly as in a competitive labour market.

Key Exam Tips

1

Always establish which market structure (Section 3.4) or labour market type (competitive vs monopsony, Sections 22–23) a scenario describes before choosing a diagram or predicting a policy effect — the correct answer to "what happens to employment/price/output" depends entirely on this initial classification.

2

Distinguish normal profit from supernormal profit explicitly (Section 10) in any answer discussing long-run market structure outcomes — confusing the two is a frequently penalised error.

3

For 12-mark and 25-mark questions, ensure every knowledge/application/analysis point is explicitly applied to the specific context given (a named firm, industry, or policy) — generic theory without contextual application loses significant application marks under Edexcel's mark scheme.

4

Remember that Edexcel's mark schemes reward well-developed evaluation highly — always end extended answers with a explicit reasoned judgement (which effect is more significant, and why) rather than simply listing points for and against.

5

For price discrimination and monopsony diagrams, always label every curve (AR, MR, MC, AC, S=AC, D=MRP as appropriate) and every price/quantity point precisely — partial labelling is a common way marks are lost even when the underlying analysis is correct.

6

When discussing government intervention, always weigh the specific benefit against a specific limitation (asymmetric information, regulatory capture, administrative cost, or unintended consequences, Section 27) rather than treating intervention as costless.

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | Edexcel A-Level Economics A | Theme 3: Business Behaviour and the Labour Market
